v.t: Yuan Dynasty official maritime transport headed by Zhu Qing and Zhang Xuan
v.s: To alleviate grain supply shortages in Dadu (present-day Beijing), the Yuan court appointed Zhu Qing and Zhang Xuan as maritime transport commissioners to organize large-scale sea-based grain shipping. They sailed from Liujiagang (Taicang, Jiangsu) to Zhigu (Tianjin), then transferred to Dadu. Annual shipments grew from tens of thousands to hundreds of thousands of shi, greatly lowering transport costs and boosting coastal port cities. This move also produced China's earliest seafaring lineages.

v.t: Dante begins exile and conceives The Divine Comedy
v.s: Florentine political strife intensified; Dante's White Guelph party lost power due to papal and French royal intervention. In 1301 the Black Guelphs seized Florence, and the following year Dante was sentenced to perpetual exile on charges of corruption and opposing the pope, with his property confiscated on pain of execution by fire. Dante never returned to his hometown and, during his exile, began composing the epic poem The Divine Comedy, turning his suffering into an immortal literary masterpiece.

v.t: First War of Scottish Independence: Battle of Fife
v.s: Scottish nobles resisted English King Edward I's suppression. John de Soulis led Scottish forces against the English in Fife. Though tactically inconclusive, the battle bolstered Scottish resistance, laying the groundwork for Robert the Bruce's later uprising. At this time Scotland was caught between English military occupation and national resistance.

v.t: Golden Horde and Ilkhanate call a truce
v.s: The two Mongol khanates had fought for decades over the Caucasus. In 1302, Toqta Khan of the Golden Horde and Ghazan Khan of the Ilkhanate agreed to a ceasefire, temporarily dividing spheres of influence in Azerbaijan. The peace partly restored security on the western Silk Road, promoting a brief boom in East-West trade.

v.t: First meeting of the French Estates-General
v.s: To levy new taxes for the war in Flanders, King Philip IV of France convened representatives of the clergy, nobility, and commoners at Notre-Dame de Paris. This was France's first nationwide representative assembly. Though limited in power at the time, it set a precedent for consulting society and paralleled the development of the English Parliament.

v.t: Battle of the Golden Spurs: Flemish militia defeat French knights
v.s: Flemish urban militias (mainly Bruges textile workers) used muddy terrain and pike formations to miraculously defeat France's elite heavy knights at Courtrai. The battle marked a revival of infantry tactics, weakened French control in the Low Countries, and spurred Flemish urban autonomy.

v.t: Yuan Dynasty issues the Da Yuan Tong Zhi (Great Yuan Comprehensive Institutions)
v.s: Emperor Chengzong ordered ministers to compile the regulations issued over the years. Completed in 1303, the Da Yuan Tong Zhi contained 2,539 articles, including imperial edicts, administrative rules, and precedents. It was the second major legal code after the Zhiyuan Xinge, and though never fully implemented, remains a key source for Yuan legal history.

v.t: Pope Boniface VIII dies after humiliation by the French king
v.s: King Philip IV of France clashed with Pope Boniface VIII over taxing the clergy. Philip sent troops to attack the pope's residence at Anagni, imprisoning him and preparing a trial. Although local residents rescued him, Boniface died of shock and humiliation in October. This event marked a severe decline in papal authority, with royal power rising above ecclesiastical power.

v.t: Siege of Stirling Castle, Scotland
v.s: Edward I besieged Stirling Castle, Scotland's last stronghold. The garrison held out for four months before surrendering due to starvation. The English king massacred most of the defenders and fortified the castle as a military hub. However, Scottish noble Robert the Bruce did not join the battle, secretly gathering strength for an uprising.

v.t: Yuan Dynasty and Pagan Kingdom of Myanmar make peace
v.s: The Yuan had repeatedly campaigned against the Pagan Kingdom since 1277 at great cost. In 1304 the two sides agreed: Pagan became a Yuan tributary vassal, while the Yuan withdrew most troops. Thereafter, Myanmar fragmented into Shan, Mon, and other small states until the Ava Kingdom unified it in the mid‑14th century.

v.t: William Wallace executed in London
v.s: Scottish independence leader William Wallace was betrayed in 1304 and taken to London. On 23 August 1305, he was hanged, drawn, and quartered at Smithfield for treason. His heroic death instead fueled stronger Scottish resistance, allowing Robert the Bruce to rise and eventually win Scottish independence.

v.t: French king Philip IV begins preparing the suppression of the Knights Templar
v.s: Facing financial crisis, Philip IV coveted the immense wealth of the Knights Templar and began gathering 'heretical' charges against them. In 1305 he secretly ordered officials to investigate the knights' conduct, setting the stage for the mass arrests of 1307. This typified European monarchs' attacks on religious military orders.

v.t: Robert the Bruce kills rival and is crowned King of Scotland
v.s: Scottish noble Robert the Bruce quarrelled with rival John Comyn in the Greyfriars Church and stabbed him. Comyn was an English supporter, and Bruce took drastic action to remove an obstacle. He then hurried to Scone and was crowned King of Scotland as Robert I on 25 March 1306. Edward I was furious and sent troops, forcing Bruce into temporary exile.

v.t: Yuan Dynasty bans private possession of weapons among Han Chinese
v.s: To prevent Han and Southern Chinese uprisings, the Yuan government issued a decree confiscating weapons, stipulating that private possession of crossbows, arrows, and armor would be punished by law. In practice, southern clans and rebels still hid weapons, exacerbating social tensions and sowing seeds for the later Red Turban Rebellion.

v.t: Emperor Chengzong dies; Emperor Wuzong ascends amid court struggle
v.s: Emperor Chengzong (Temür) died childless in February 1307. Empress Bulugan tried to install Ananda, Prince of Anxi. Khayishan (Emperor Wuzong), supported by his brother Ayurbarwada, led troops south from Mongolia, seized the throne, imprisoned Bulugan, and killed Ananda. In June, Khayishan was enthroned in Shangdu. Frequent succession conflicts weakened the Yuan's ruling foundation.

v.t: Mass arrest of the Knights Templar
v.s: On Friday, 13 October 1307, King Philip IV of France suddenly ordered the arrest of all Knights Templar in France on charges of heresy, sodomy, and idolatry. Hundreds of knights were seized, and their property confiscated. Under torture, many, including Grand Master Jacques de Molay, 'confessed'. This event is one origin of the 'Friday the 13th' superstition.

v.t: House of Luxembourg established in the Holy Roman Empire
v.s: After Holy Roman Emperor Albert I was murdered by his nephew, the seven prince-electors chose Count Henry of Luxembourg as king (Henry VII). He sought to end internal chaos, strengthen central authority, and planned a march into Italy for imperial coronation. The House of Luxembourg replaced the Habsburgs, opening a new era.

v.t: Japan's Kamakura shogunate suppresses Emperor Go-Daigo's anti-shogunate plot
v.s: After Emperor Go-Daigo ascended the throne, he secretly contacted anti-shogunate warriors and courtiers, attempting to restore direct imperial rule. In 1308, the Kamakura shogunate discovered his plans and placed close aides like Hino Toshisuke under house arrest, forcing the emperor to postpone action. This abortive plot, called the Shōchū Incident, foreshadowed the Nanboku-chō period.

v.t: University of Paris receives papal charter
v.s: Pope Clement V, at the request of the French king, formally recognised the University of Paris as a Studium Generale, granting it the right to award degrees and strike. The university became Europe's most famous centre for theology and philosophy, attracting scholars such as Thomas Aquinas and Duns Scotus, and producing the medieval intellectual elite.

v.t: Papacy moves to Avignon – the 'Babylonian Captivity' begins
v.s: Under pressure from King Philip IV of France, the newly elected Pope Clement V moved the papal court from Rome to Avignon. For the next 68 years (1309-1377), seven successive popes were French and under French control. This 'Avignon Captivity' severely undermined papal authority, reduced the papacy to a French political tool, and later led to the Western Schism.

v.t: Emperor Wuzong mints 'Zhida Tongbao' and reforms currency
v.s: To solve fiscal problems, Emperor Wuzong ordered the minting of 'Zhida Tongbao' copper coins to circulate alongside paper money. At the same time, he mass-issued 'Zhida Silver Notes', which caused inflation due to insufficient reserves. The currency reform lasted only three years, reflecting the Yuan's chaotic financial management.

v.t: First mechanical clock tower built in Milan, Europe
v.s: The church of San Gottardo in Milan built Europe's earliest public mechanical clock, driven by gears and a weight, striking the hour. This invention shifted the habit of marking time by prayer bells, promoting precision in urban life. Large clock towers thereafter became standard in European cities, facilitating commercial regulation.

v.t: Mali Empire expands to Gao
v.s: Mansa (king) Abubakari II of the Mali Empire conquered Gao, controlled by the Songhai, incorporating it into the empire. Mali now controlled the gold and salt trade routes along the middle Niger, growing in power. This expansion set the stage for Mansa Musa's legendary hajj and the empire's golden age.

v.t: Emperor Wuzong honours Confucius
v.s: To win support from Han Chinese scholar-officials, Emperor Wuzong issued an edict posthumously granting Confucius the title 'Great Perfected Ultimate Sage and Manifest King', renovated the Temple of Confucius, and elevated the status of Confucian scholars. He also started preparatory work to restore the imperial examination system. These measures eased Mongol-Han tensions but did not fundamentally change the discriminatory four-class system.

v.t: Emperor Wuzong dies; Emperor Renzong succeeds and repeals the new policies
v.s: Emperor Wuzong died in January 1311 at age 30, due to excessive drinking and sex. His younger brother Ayurbarwada (Emperor Renzong), supported by Empress Dowager Dagi, took the throne. He immediately cancelled the Zhida Silver Notes and copper coins, halted new capital construction, and restored the old system of Chengzong. Renzong, who revered Confucianism and employed Han Chinese officials, opened a period of relative stability.

v.t: Council of Vienne discusses the aftermath of the Knights Templar
v.s: Pope Clement V convened an ecumenical council at Vienne. Under pressure from the French king, the council formally dissolved the Knights Templar and transferred their property to the Knights Hospitaller. Grand Master Jacques de Molay was sentenced to life imprisonment and later burned at the stake in 1314. The council also discussed the failures of the Crusades but made no substantive progress.

v.t: Council of Vienne officially disbands the Knights Templar
v.s: After years of trials and pressure, the Council of Vienne issued the papal bull 'Vox in excelso', formally dissolving the Knights Templar. All their property was transferred to the Hospitallers; members were either imprisoned or moved to other orders. The Templars, a prominent military order for two centuries, disappeared completely. European monarchs' attacks on religious powers reached a peak.

v.t: Completion of the 'Sakuteiki' (Records of Garden Making) in Japan
v.s: Japanese garden master Musō Soseki synthesised Zen garden principles in the 'Sakuteiki', emphasising the fusion of nature and spirit. His design of Saihō-ji temple garden influenced later karesansui (dry landscape) styles. The book became an important theoretical classic for Japanese garden art.

v.t: Mali Empire conquers the Songhai chief
v.s: Mali imperial forces attacked the Songhai chief, forcing him into submission. The Songhai retained internal autonomy but paid tribute to Mali. This conquest gave Mali control over more distant salt and copper trade routes, further raising its power.

v.t: Emperor Renzong issues edict to restore the imperial examination
v.s: Acting on Confucian ministers' advice, Emperor Renzong ordered the restoration of the imperial examinations, setting the prefectural exam for 1314 and the metropolitan exam for 1315. The exams used a 'left‑right separate list', with Mongols and Semu on one list and Han and Southern Chinese on the other, but quotas were heavily skewed. This was the only truly implemented imperial examination during the Yuan, a great boon to Han scholar-officials.

v.t: Scandinavian 'Erik's Chronicle' completed
v.s: The Swedish knight Erik wrote Sweden's first major rhymed chronicle in Old Swedish, recounting Swedish history from 1229 to 1319, especially the deeds of the Sten Sture family. It reflects the awakening of Swedish national consciousness in the late Middle Ages.

v.t: Battle of Bannockburn: Scotland crushes England
v.s: King Robert the Bruce of Scotland met the huge army of England's Edward II in the marshes of Bannockburn, using pike schiltrons. The English cavalry got bogged down and were slaughtered by Scottish spearmen. Edward II fled to Dunbar, and the English suffered heavy casualties. The battle effectively secured Scottish independence and cemented Robert the Bruce as a national hero.

v.t: First Yuan prefectural examination held
v.s: The first prefectural examination after the restoration was held simultaneously in 17 provinces of the Yuan. About 300 Han and Southern Chinese passed, ready to join Mongol and Semu candidates in the following year's metropolitan exam. Although quotas favoured Mongols and Semu, scholars still viewed it as a path to advancement.

v.t: King Louis X of France frees serfs
v.s: To increase royal revenue and win popular favour, King Louis X issued an edict freeing serfs on royal lands, allowing them to buy their freedom or gain liberty through settlement. Though limited to royal estates, the measure advanced the liberation of French peasants.

v.t: Great Famine breaks out in Europe
v.s: Continuous heavy rain caused grain crop failure across Europe, from Ireland to Poland, leading to widespread famine. Between 1315 and 1317, an estimated 10–25% of the population died, with reports of cannibalism. The famine weakened Europe's population and contributed to later susceptibility to the Black Death. It also intensified social unrest and anti-Semitic violence.

v.t: Swiss three-canton alliance against the Habsburgs
v.s: The Swiss forest cantons (Uri, Schwyz, Unterwalden) allied with Lucerne and Zurich to resist Habsburg encroachment. They signed the Treaty of Brunnen, agreeing to internal arbitration. This laid the foundation for the expansion of the Swiss Confederacy.

v.t: Yuan metropolitan and palace examinations successfully held
v.s: Fifty-six candidates (including 32 Mongols and Semu, and 24 Han and Southern Chinese) passed the metropolitan exam and attended the palace exam, where Emperor Renzong personally posed questions. In the end, 56 jinshi (including Khutughtu) were selected. This was the high point of the Yuan examinations. Although later interrupted, the Renzong era's examinations were crucial for transmitting Han culture.

v.t: Emperor Renzong appoints crown prince, causing uncle‑nephew conflict
v.s: Emperor Renzong broke the agreement with his brother Emperor Wuzong (after a brother‑to‑brother succession, the throne should pass to Wuzong's son) and made his own son, Shidébāla (later Emperor Yingzong), crown prince. This disgruntled Wuzong's old followers and two princes, planting seeds for the later Nanpo Incident.

v.t: Famine crisis in Ireland
v.s: The Great Famine reached Ireland. The English king's lord lieutenant in Ireland could not cope; starvation occurred in Dublin and elsewhere. England itself was overwhelmed, and Ireland fell into anarchy.

v.t: Death of King Louis X of France; birth and death of posthumous son John I
v.s: Louis X died suddenly, leaving his widow Clemence pregnant. His brother Philip acted as regent, and when the child was born a boy, he was proclaimed John I. But John I lived only five days, after which Philip took the throne as Philip V. This episode pushed French succession rules toward the Salic Law.

v.t: Yuan establishes the 'Du Gongde Shisi' to manage Buddhism
v.s: Emperor Renzong created the Bureau of Meritorious Deeds to unify management of Buddhist affairs and clergy, under the Imperial Preceptor or the Bureau of Buddhist and Tibetan Affairs. This strengthened Yuan control over Tibetan Buddhism but increased clerical corruption.

v.t: Scotland recaptures Berwick-upon-Tweed
v.s: Scottish forces under Robert the Bruce captured Berwick, England's most important stronghold in Scotland. Scotland was now almost completely liberated. Bruce began rebuilding a Scottish navy and raiding northern England.

v.t: Yuan reopens the Maritime Trade Bureau in Guangdong
v.s: The Yuan reopened the maritime trade bureaus in Guangzhou, Quanzhou, and Ningbo to encourage overseas trade. Merchants from Persia, Arabia, and Southeast Asia flocked to Quanzhou, making Zayton a world‑class port.

v.t: Yuan ends war with the Ögedei Khanate
v.s: Emperor Renzong sent envoys to make peace with Yesün Temür, leader of the Ögedei Khanate. Both sides recognised the Chagatai Khanate's sphere of influence north of the Amu Darya, and the Yuan gave up direct control of Central Asia. The Silk Road regained security, and East‑West diplomatic exchanges increased.

v.t: Dante completes the final canticle of The Divine Comedy, 'Paradiso'
v.s: Dante finished 'Paradiso' in exile, completing his epic poem. He used the Tuscan vernacular to depict the soul's ascent to heaven, with his beloved Beatrice as guide. The Divine Comedy became the foundational work of Italian literature.

v.t: Poland reunifies the kingdom
v.s: Władysław I the Elbow-high was crowned King of Poland, ending two centuries of fragmentation. Through marriage and conquest, he united Greater Poland and Lesser Poland and made Kraków the capital. The revival of the Polish kingdom provided national strength to resist the Teutonic Knights.

v.t: Emperor Renzong dies; Emperor Yingzong succeeds
v.s: Emperor Renzong (Ayurbarwada) died at age 35. Crown Prince Shidébāla (Emperor Yingzong) ascended the throne in Shangdu. At only 17, Yingzong was dominated by his grandmother, Empress Dowager Dagi, and the powerful minister Temüder, opening a brief period of reform attempts.

v.t: Formation of the Persian miniature painting school
v.s: The Tabriz school, under Mongol Ilkhanate patronage, created illustrations for the 'Compendium of Chronicles' (Jami' al-tawarikh), blending Chinese gongbi style with Persian tradition, inaugurating the golden age of Persian miniature painting. This style influenced the entire Islamic world.

v.t: Dante dies in Ravenna
v.s: The Italian poet Dante died in his place of exile, Ravenna, at age 56. He was buried in the church of San Francesco. Florence repeatedly requested the return of his remains, but was refused. The Divine Comedy had already spread throughout Italy, and Petrarch called him 'the first modern poet'.

v.t: Emperor Yingzong purges Temüder's faction
v.s: After taking personal rule, Emperor Yingzong cracked down on the powerful minister Temüder and his clique, executing Temüder's son Suonan and others. He promoted Confucian official Baiju and carried out the 'Zhizhi New Policies', reducing the number of officials and rectifying administration. This was the most promising reform of the late Yuan.

v.t: Emperor Yingzong issues a revised edition of the Da Yuan Tong Zhi
v.s: Emperor Yingzong ordered ministers including Baiju to recodify the Da Yuan Tong Zhi, adding new cases and deleting outdated clauses, completing the work in 1322. This code paid more attention to uniformity in criminal law, but its implementation remained limited.

v.t: Alchemical text 'Rosarium philosophorum' circulates in Europe
v.s: Franciscan friar Johannes de Rupescissa compiled the 'Rosary of the Philosophers', detailing alchemical techniques such as distillation and calcination in the quest for the philosopher's stone. The book became a standard alchemy textbook in medieval Europe, indirectly advancing experimental chemistry.

v.t: Nanpo Incident: Emperor Yingzong assassinated
v.s: Emperor Yingzong and Baiju were assassinated at Nanpo (present-day Zhenglan Banner, Inner Mongolia) by conservative nobles led by Tegshi and Yesün Temür. Yingzong was only 21. The conspirators enthroned Yingzong's uncle, Yesün Temür (Emperor Taiding). This coup completely crushed the Yuan's sinicisation reforms, and thereafter the Yuan fell into internal strife.

v.t: Norway signs peace treaty ending civil war
v.s: King Magnus VII of Norway concluded a treaty with the opposition, confirming the royal succession law and ending 20 years of dynastic strife. Norway then entered a union with Sweden, and its power declined.

v.t: Mansa Musa of Mali embarks on his epic hajj to Mecca
v.s: Mansa Musa, emperor of Mali, led about 60,000 people (including 12,000 slaves) and 80 camels laden with gold from Niani to Mecca. Passing through Cairo, his lavish spending caused gold prices to drop by 20%; thereafter Europe regarded Mali as a land of gold. The hajj showcased West Africa's wealth, and the 1375 Catalan Atlas depicts Musa holding a golden scepter.

v.t: Marco Polo dies in Venice
v.s: The famous traveller Marco Polo died in Venice, leaving behind 'The Travels of Marco Polo'. On his deathbed, friends urged him to delete 'exaggerations' from the book; he replied, 'I have not told half of what I saw and heard.' His work inspired European curiosity about the East and indirectly spurred the Age of Discovery.

v.t: Emperor Taiding favours the Hui people
v.s: To win support from the Semu aristocracy, Emperor Taiding issued an edict protecting Islam, ordering the construction of mosques, and entrusting finance to Hui Muslims. In the mid‑and‑late Yuan, Hui merchants became powerful and monopolised maritime trade.

v.t: Aztecs found Tenochtitlan
v.s: The Mexica (Aztec precursors) saw the prophetic sign of an eagle eating a snake while perched on a cactus on a small island in Lake Texcoco and there founded Tenochtitlan (now Mexico City). They built chinampas (artificial islands), causeways, and canals, turning it into the largest city in Mesoamerica.

v.t: Grand Prince of Moscow crowned Grand Prince of All Russia
v.s: Ivan I (Kalita), Grand Prince of Moscow, received the title 'Grand Prince of Vladimir and All Russia' from the Golden Horde, along with the right to collect tribute. He used this authority to absorb neighbouring principalities, laying the foundation for the rise of Russia.

v.t: Yuan bans Han Chinese from bearing bows and arrows
v.s: In 1325 (Taiding 2), the Yuan issued a strict decree forbidding Han Chinese and Southern Chinese from possessing bows, arrows, or weapons. As a Mongol minority regime, the Yuan enforced severe weapons controls over the Han. The ban, however, could not prevent increasing peasant uprisings and smuggling.

v.t: Foundation of Tenochtitlan: Beginning of the Aztec Empire
v.s: In 1325 the Aztecs (Mexica) founded Tenochtitlan on a small island in Lake Texcoco (present-day Mexico City), following the sign of an eagle eating a snake on a cactus. Through the Triple Alliance (with Texcoco and Tlacopan), the Aztecs built the most powerful empire in Mesoamerica. By the time the Spanish arrived (1519), Tenochtitlan had about 200,000 inhabitants, making it one of the world's largest cities.

v.t: Orhan, son of Osman, captures Bursa and makes it the capital
v.s: After Osman I died, his son Orhan captured the Byzantine city of Brusa (Bursa) and made it the Ottoman capital. Orhan established regular infantry (yaya) and cavalry (müsellem), minted silver coins, and transformed the Ottoman state from a frontier principality into a true state.

v.t: Earthquake in Sichuan, Yuan Dynasty
v.s: A strong earthquake struck Sichuan, collapsing buildings in Chengdu, Chongqing, and other cities, killing tens of thousands. Emperor Taiding ordered relief and tax exemptions, but local officials embezzled the funds, fueling popular resentment.

v.t: Edward III of England assumes personal rule
v.s: Edward III, aged 18, declared his majority and overthrew the regency of his mother Isabella and her lover Roger Mortimer. He executed Mortimer and exiled his mother, beginning to display his qualities as a great king. Edward III later launched the Hundred Years' War, reshaping English kingship.

v.t: Last Yuan imperial examination metropolitan exam
v.s: Emperor Taiding held the imperial examinations, enrolling 86 jinshi. Thereafter, due to frequent internal conflicts, the examinations were intermittent, and never regained the scale of the Renzong era.

v.t: War of the Two Capitals: Yuan imperial civil war
v.s: Emperor Taiding died. In Dadu, El Temür enthroned Tugh Temür (Emperor Wenzong), a son of Khayishan, while in Shangdu, Daolat Shah enthroned Taiding's young son Aragibag (Emperor Tianshun). The two sides fought a civil war. El Temür captured Shangdu, and Emperor Tianshun disappeared. Wenzong won, but his rule rested on a weak foundation.

v.t: House of Valois replaces Capetian dynasty in France
v.s: King Charles IV of France died without an heir, ending the direct Capetian line. Philip of Valois, a cousin, took the throne as Philip VI, founding the House of Valois. King Edward III of England, citing his maternal descent, claimed the French throne, laying the fuse for the Hundred Years' War.

v.t: Emperor Wenzong poisons his brother Emperor Mingzong to regain the throne
v.s: Under public pressure, Emperor Wenzong had welcomed his older brother Kuśala (Emperor Mingzong) as emperor, but Mingzong died suddenly after only seven days, believed poisoned by Wenzong and El Temür. Wenzong resumed the throne, massacring many of Mingzong's old officials. This 'Tianli Incident' utterly corrupted Yuan imperial morality.

v.t: Mansa Musa returns from hajj and launches major building projects
v.s: On his return from Mecca, Mansa Musa brought back Arab scholars and architects. He built the Djinguereber Mosque and the Sankore University in Timbuktu, making it a centre of Islamic scholarship in West Africa.

v.t: Complete renovation of the Grand Canal under the Yuan
v.s: Emperor Wenzong ordered the dredging of the Huitong River and Tonghui River, building locks to regulate water levels, making the Grand Canal fully navigable. The cost of shipping grain from south to north was greatly reduced, Dadu's grain supply was secured, and canal cities like Linqing and Jizhou flourished.

v.t: Double‑entry bookkeeping appears in Italy
v.s: Florentine bankers adopted debit/credit double‑entry bookkeeping and popularised Arabic numerals. This accounting revolution enabled merchants to calculate profits and losses precisely, fostering the emergence of capitalism.

v.t: Grand Duke Gediminas of Lithuania founds Vilnius
v.s: Grand Duke Gediminas of Lithuania founded the new city of Vilnius on a hunting ground as a centre to unify the Lithuanian tribes. Using flexible diplomacy with Poland and the Teutonic Knights, he expanded his territory.

v.t: Emperor Go-Daigo's anti‑shogunate plot fails and he is exiled
v.s: Emperor Go-Daigo launched a second anti‑shogunate plot (the Genkō Incident), but the Kamakura shogunate discovered it. The shogunate exiled him to Oki Island and installed Emperor Kōgon. Although the plot failed, discontent with the shogunate spread among the warrior class.

v.t: King Stefan Dušan of Serbia usurps the throne
v.s: Stefan Dušan overthrew his father Stefan Uroš III and seized the throne. He then dramatically reformed the army, invaded the Byzantine Empire, and soon established the Serbian Empire.

v.t: Emperor Wenzong dies; strongman El Temür installs a child emperor
v.s: Emperor Wenzong died, leaving a will to pass the throne to a son of Emperor Mingzong. But the powerful minister El Temür, to perpetuate his power, enthroned the seven‑year‑old Rinchinbal (Emperor Ningzong). After only 43 days, Ningzong died. El Temür then enthroned Mingzong's eldest son, Toghon Temür (Emperor Shundi), and the Yuan entered its final years.

v.t: Scotland‑France 'Auld Alliance' renewed
v.s: Scotland and France signed the Treaty of Verdun, reaffirming mutual support against England. France provided military aid to Scotland, while Scotland harassed northern England. The alliance lasted for centuries.

v.t: Emperor Go-Daigo restored; Kamakura shogunate falls
v.s: Emperor Go-Daigo escaped from Oki Island and called on warriors to overthrow the shogunate. Ashikaga Takauji and Kusunoki Masashige defeated the Kamakura shogunate's forces; Nitta Yoshisada captured Kamakura, and the Hōjō clan committed suicide. Go-Daigo returned to Kyōto and began the 'Kenmu Restoration'.

v.t: Emperor Shundi ascends; El Temür dominates the court
v.s: Emperor Shundi (Toghon Temür) came to the throne at age 13, while the powerful minister El Temür continued to monopolise power, living in debauchery and allowing government to decay. Shundi bided his time, waiting to eliminate the minister.

v.t: Medici family rises in Florence
v.s: Giovanni di Bicci de' Medici founded the Medici Bank, amassing wealth through wool trade and banking, becoming the richest man in Florence. He patronised artists, built the San Lorenzo church, and began the Medici's domination of Florence.

v.t: White Lotus rebellion in Shunde, Yuan
v.s: White Lotus followers rose in Shunde, Hebei, killing officials and burning offices, but were quickly suppressed. This was a prelude to the massive peasant uprisings at the end of the Yuan. Han Shantong and Liu Futong gained experience from it.

v.t: Ashikaga Takauji rebels against Emperor Go-Daigo, establishing the Ashikaga shogunate
v.s: Dissatisfied with the Kenmu Restoration's favouritism toward court nobles, Ashikaga Takauji raised an army, captured Kyōto, and forced Go-Daigo to flee to Yoshino, creating a Northern and Southern Courts period. Takauji installed Emperor Kōmyō in Kyōto, was appointed Seii Taishōgun, and formally established the Ashikaga shogunate (1338).

v.t: Hungarian‑Polish alliance against Austria
v.s: King Charles I of Hungary and King Casimir III of Poland allied against the expansion of the Holy Roman Empire's House of Luxembourg. The alliance strengthened their positions in southeast Europe.

v.t: Vijayanagara Empire founded – the last Hindu bastion
v.s: Harihara I and Bukka I founded the Vijayanagara Empire in southern India, establishing its capital at Vijayanagara (present-day Hampi). The empire championed Hinduism against Muslim invasions from the northern Delhi Sultanate, preserving Hindu culture.

v.t: King Philip VI of France declares confiscation of English territories in France
v.s: King Philip VI of France, citing Edward III's sheltering of French fugitives, declared the confiscation of Gascony and other English lands in France. Edward III then claimed the French throne for himself, igniting the Hundred Years' War.

v.t: Hundred Years' War begins
v.s: King Edward III of England invaded France, marking the start of the Hundred Years' War (1337–1453). He first captured Flanders, won the naval Battle of Sluys, and proclaimed himself King of France. England gained the upper hand; battles like Crécy and Poitiers devastated France.

v.t: Emperor Shundi dismisses Bayan, takes personal control
v.s: The powerful minister Bayan had monopolised power for three years, acting arbitrarily and even plotting to depose the emperor. With the support of Toqto'a and others, Emperor Shundi engineered a coup, dismissed Bayan, and exiled him to death. Shundi began ruling personally, appointing Toqto'a as chancellor and launching the 'Zhizheng Reforms' to save the dynasty.

v.t: Preliminary Golden Bull of the Holy Roman Empire
v.s: Emperor Louis IV and the prince‑electors concluded the Rhenish League Treaty, affirming the exclusive right of the seven electors to choose the emperor, laying the groundwork for the Golden Bull of 1356. Imperial central authority continued to fragment.

v.t: Yuan opens river control and holds metropolitan examination
v.s: In 1338 (Zhiyuan 4), Emperor Shundi ordered river control (Yellow River regulation) and held the metropolitan exam. Since the restoration under Yanyou, the Yuan exams had been intermittent. The 1338 exam enrolled about 100 jinshi, but by the late Yuan, political corruption had made the exam's role in selecting officials limited.

v.t: Yellow River breaches at Baimaokou; Yuan begins control works
v.s: The Yellow River burst its banks at Baimaokou (near present-day Cao County, Shandong), flooding vast areas and affecting grain transport. The Yuan sent Minister of Works Cheng Zun to manage the breach, but the enormous cost burdened the population, contributing to the Red Turban rebellion.

v.t: English Parliament passes statute limiting papal power
v.s: Edward III, seeking to free England from papal control, passed the Statute of Provisors, forbidding appeals to the Pope and papal appointments of bishops. This was an important step toward the independence of the Church of England.

v.t: Battle of Sluys: English destroy French fleet
v.s: The first major naval battle of the Hundred Years' War. King Edward III of England, with about 200 ships, annihilated the combined French and Genoese fleet off the port of Sluys. The French lost almost their entire fleet, and England gained control of the English Channel.

v.t: Emperor Shundi changes era name to Zhizheng; Toqto'a begins reforms
v.s: Emperor Shundi abolished the 'Zhiyuan' era name and changed it to 'Zhizheng', appointing Toqto'a as right chancellor. Toqto'a launched the 'Zhizheng Reforms': restoring the imperial examinations, reducing redundant officials, overturning unjust cases, and issuing new paper money (Zhizheng Jiaochao). This was known as the 'Zhizheng Renewal'.

v.t: Petrarch receives the laurel crown in Rome
v.s: Italian poet Petrarch was crowned with the laurel wreath on the Capitoline Hill in Rome, the first such honour since classical antiquity. He vigorously promoted humanism, collected classical manuscripts, and is known as the 'Father of Humanism'.

v.t: Civil strife in the Tughlaq dynasty of the Delhi Sultanate
v.s: In 1341, civil unrest broke out in the Tughlaq dynasty of the Delhi Sultanate. A series of controversial reforms by Sultan Muhammad bin Tughluq (including token currency and shifting the capital) failed, leading to rebellions across the empire. Bengal and other regions seceded, and the empire's territory shrank dramatically.

v.t: Louis the Great ascends the Hungarian throne, a great power of Eastern Europe
v.s: King Charles I of Hungary died; his son Louis the Great (Louis I) succeeded. He later also inherited the Polish crown, creating a Hungarian‑Polish union and repeatedly defeating Venice and the Ottomans.

v.t: Yuan begins compilation of the official histories of Liao, Jin, and Song
v.s: Toqto'a petitioned Emperor Shundi to establish a history bureau and begin the official compilation of the histories of the Liao, Jin, and Song dynasties. Toqto'a insisted that each dynasty be treated as legitimate, breaking the traditional bias that favoured Song over Liao and Jin.

v.t: Prelude to the Red Turban rebellion: White Lotus secret activities
v.s: White Lotus leaders Han Shantong and Liu Futong buried a one‑eyed stone man near the Yellow River construction site, spreading the rhyme: 'One‑eyed stone man, stir the Yellow River, and all under Heaven will rebel.' The uprising was imminent.

v.t: Hundred Years' War: War of the Breton Succession
v.s: In 1343, during the Hundred Years' War, the War of the Breton Succession was under way. Duke John III of Brittany died without issue; England and France supported rival claimants. England backed John de Montfort, France backed Charles of Blois, leading to a protracted struggle in Brittany.

v.t: Petrarch discovers the letters of Cicero
v.s: In the cathedral library of Verona, Petrarch discovered the collected letters of Cicero to Atticus, filled with excitement. These letters revealed the political intrigues of the late Roman Republic and became a key document of Renaissance classical scholarship.

v.t: Toqto'a resigns; Yellow River flooding worsens
v.s: In 1344 (Zhizheng 4), the Yellow River breached its banks at Caozhou and Jining, flooding hundreds of li and destroying countless farms and villages. Right Chancellor Toqto'a, due to conflicts with Emperor Shundi, was forced to resign, leaving river control unattended. The court, short of funds, could not provide effective relief, and refugees wandered. The flooding and inadequate relief intensified social conflicts, leading to widespread uprisings. Toqto'a returned in 1351 to lead river control.

v.t: War between Venice and Genoa (1345)
v.s: In 1345, the two Italian maritime republics, Venice and Genoa, went to war over control of Black Sea trade. The war continued until 1348. Venice won a pyrrhic victory, but both sides suffered heavy losses from the Black Death. The war weakened both republics' trading positions in the Black Sea.

v.t: Yuan completes the three official histories
v.s: The three dynastic histories – History of Liao, History of Jin, and History of Song – were completed after two and a half years, totalling about 500 juan. Toqto'a served as chief director. They are the last three of the Twenty‑Four Histories, though the History of Song is often criticised as verbose and error‑prone.

v.t: Battle of Crécy: English longbows decimate French knights
v.s: A major battle of the Hundred Years' War. Edward III's English longbowmen won a stunning victory at Crécy against Philip VI's French army. French casualties numbered about 15,000, English fewer than 200. Firearms were used for the first time on a European battlefield. The battle marked the end of the age of heavy cavalry.

v.t: Fang Guozhen's rebellion in Yuan, curtain‑raiser to the end‑of‑Yuan peasant wars
v.s: Fang Guozhen, a man from Taizhou, was falsely accused by an enemy, killed his accuser, and fled to sea, gathering several thousand followers to raid grain transport. The Yuan expeditions against him repeatedly failed. This was the first large‑scale rebellion of the late Yuan, setting off a wave of nationwide uprisings.

v.t: Black Death reaches Sicily from Caffa, Europe's nightmare begins
v.s: During the siege of the Genoese trading port of Caffa, the Mongol Golden Horde used catapults to hurl plague‑ridden corpses into the city. Surviving Genoese merchants fled to Messina, Sicily, bringing the Black Death to Europe. Over the next few years, it killed an estimated 25 million people (one‑third of Europe's population), upending European social structure.

v.t: Yuan appoints Toqto'a as Grand Tutor and restores him as chancellor
v.s: In 1347 (Zhizheng 7), Emperor Shundi reappointed Toqto'a as right chancellor and Grand Tutor. Toqto'a had been dismissed earlier due to political struggles. After returning, he introduced some reform measures, including overseeing the compilation of the Liao, Jin, and Song histories and controlling the Yellow River. However, due to political corruption and fiscal collapse, the reforms had little effect.

v.t: Boccaccio begins writing The Decameron
v.s: During the plague in Florence, Boccaccio began writing The Decameron, using the frame of ten young people fleeing the city and telling 100 stories. The work satirises church corruption and celebrates worldly love, becoming a model of Italian prose.

v.t: Yuan issues Zhizheng Jiaochao, hyperinflation erupts
v.s: To resolve fiscal crisis, the Yuan massively printed paper money, causing depreciation and soaring prices. The populace refused to use paper money, reverting to barter. Economic collapse accelerated the Yuan's downfall.

v.t: Peak of anti‑Jewish pogroms in Europe (Valentine's Day massacre)
v.s: During the Black Death, European Christians blamed Jews for poisoning wells. On St. Valentine's Day, mass executions of Jews occurred in Strasbourg and elsewhere; about 2,000 were killed. Many Jewish communities were destroyed, and survivors fled eastward, especially to Poland.

v.t: Toqto'a undertakes Yellow River control
v.s: Toqto'a personally directed the dredging of the Yellow River, conscripting 150,000 labourers to repair the Baimaokou breach. However, officials embezzled wages and provisions, causing deep resentment among the workers, which provided a trigger for the Red Turban Rebellion.

v.t: Ayutthaya Kingdom established in Thailand
v.s: King Ramathibodi I founded the Ayutthaya Kingdom (Siam) in the Chao Phraya River delta, making Ayutthaya his capital. He codified laws, introduced Khmer administrative systems, and later conquered Sukhothai, creating one of Thailand's longest‑lasting dynasties.

v.t: Inca Empire begins large‑scale expansion
v.s: Inca ruler Pachacuti (around this time) began reforming the army and expanding territory, conquering tribes around the Andes. The Inca transformed from a small state around Cusco into an empire spanning South America.

v.t: Yuan dismisses Chancellor Toqto'a, Hama rises to power
v.s: Toqto'a was dismissed after political enemies accused him of mishandling peasant uprisings. He was exiled to Yunnan and later killed. Thereafter, infighting intensified, and the Yuan was unable to cope with nationwide rebellions.

v.t: Red Turban Rebellion erupts, Yuan descends into chaos
v.s: White Lotus leaders Han Shantong and Liu Futong launched an uprising at Yingzhou (now Fuyang, Anhui), wearing red headscarves, hence 'Red Turban Army'. They proclaimed the slogan 'Three thousand tigers, straight to the land of Youyan', capturing several cities. Responses came from all sides; Xu Shouhui, Guo Zixing, and others rose up. Yuan rule teetered.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang becomes a Buddhist monk to avoid chaos
v.s: Famine struck Haozhou. The 17‑year‑old Zhu Yuanzhang, whose family had been wiped out, entered the Huangjue Temple as a monk. Later, lacking food at the temple, he became a wandering mendicant for three years, learning about the hardships of the people, which later helped him as a rebel leader.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang joins Guo Zixing's rebel army
v.s: Guo Zixing rebelled in Haozhou. Zhu Yuanzhang, invited by Tang He, joined as a commander of a nine‑man squad. Brave and somewhat literate, Zhu was taken as a son‑in‑law by Guo (marrying Ma Shi), thus beginning his rise.

v.t: European universities seek autonomy
v.s: After a conflict between the University of Paris and the citizens, the university petitioned the pope for privileges and obtained exemption from the city's jurisdiction. The principle of university autonomy gradually took hold, providing a foundation for academic freedom.

v.t: Zhang Shicheng proclaims himself king at Gaoyou, cutting off Yuan grain transport
v.s: Salt smuggler Zhang Shicheng rebelled in Taizhou, captured Gaoyou, and declared himself 'King Cheng' of the Great Zhou. He controlled the Grand Canal, preventing southern grain from reaching the north, causing famine in Yuan territory. The Yuan assembled a large army to besiege Gaoyou but quarrelled among themselves and withdrew.

v.t: Ottoman Empire enters Europe, occupies Gallipoli
v.s: Süleyman Pasha, son of Ottoman Sultan Orhan, led forces across the Dardanelles and captured the Gallipoli peninsula. This was the Ottomans' first permanent foothold in Europe, from which they expanded into the Balkans.

v.t: Pope Innocent VI tries to move the papacy back to Rome
v.s: Cardinal Albornoz was sent to Italy to restore papal authority, paving the way for Gregory XI's eventual return to Rome. However, the Avignon papacy remained under French control.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang captures Chuzhou, establishes a base
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang's forces took Chuzhou, using it as a centre. He disciplined his army and recruited talented men such as Li Shanchang, Xu Da, and Chang Yuchun. His power gradually grew independent of Guo Zixing.

v.t: Emperor Charles IV of the Holy Roman Empire issues the Golden Bull
v.s: At the Diets of Nuremberg and Metz, Charles IV issued the Golden Bull, specifying that the seven prince‑electors (the archbishops of Mainz, Trier, and Cologne, the King of Bohemia, the Count Palatine of the Rhine, the Duke of Saxony, and the Margrave of Brandenburg) had the exclusive right to elect the emperor. The empire thus formally recognised the elector system, and centralisation failed.

v.t: Liu Futong enthrones Han Lin'er as the 'Little King of Ming'
v.s: Liu Futong established Han Lin'er, son of Han Shantong, as emperor at Bozhou under the title 'Little King of Ming', with the dynasty name Song and era name Longfeng. The Red Turbans now had a nominal supreme leader, but local commanders did not obey. Zhu Yuanzhang would use the Longfeng era name for many years.

v.t: Battle of Poitiers: King John II of France captured
v.s: A famous battle of the Hundred Years' War. Edward the Black Prince led 6,000 English against 20,000 French, again using longbows and muddy terrain to defeat the French. King John II of France was captured; many French nobles were killed. France was forced to sign the Treaty of Brétigny, ceding large territories and paying a huge ransom.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang captures Jiqing and renames it Yingtian
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang took Jiqing (Nanjing) and renamed it Yingtian. He set up the 'Jiangnan Branch Secretariat' and called himself Duke of Wu. With Nanjing as his core, he gradually absorbed surrounding rebel and Yuan forces, becoming the strongest power in the south.

v.t: Red Turban armies launch a three‑pronged northern expedition against the Yuan
v.s: Liu Futong divided his forces into three northern columns: the eastern column under Mao Gui attacked Shandong; the central column under Guan Xian and Po Tou Pan struck Shanxi; the western column under Bai Xin attacked Shaanxi. The eastern column once approached Dadu, but the Yuan, relying on local warlords like Chaghan Temür, counterattacked, and the Red Turbans ultimately failed.

v.t: Jacquerie uprising in France
v.s: The Hundred Years' War had devastated the French countryside, leading to a massive peasant uprising (the Jacquerie, from 'jacque' meaning 'country bumpkin'). The rebels killed nobles and burned castles, but were brutally suppressed by noble forces.

v.t: Crown Prince Charles of France suppresses the Parisian revolt
v.s: Étienne Marcel, provost of the Paris merchants, led a civic uprising that forced Crown Prince Charles to flee the city. Charles gathered troops outside, retook Paris, and crushed the revolt; Marcel was assassinated. The French royal family's authority was restored.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang establishes a Lixian Pavilion to recruit literati
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang set up the Lixian Pavilion in Yingtian, inviting scholars from Zhejiang, including Liu Ji (Liu Bowen), Song Lian, Ye Chen, and Zhang Yi. Liu Ji devised the strategy: 'First destroy Chen Youliang, then eliminate Zhang Shicheng, then march north.'

v.t: Chaghan Temür recaptures Kaifeng; Red Turbans suffer a setback
v.s: Yuan general Chaghan Temür (adoptive father of Köke Temür) led his private army to capture Kaifeng, the Red Turban capital. Liu Futong and Han Lin'er fled to Anfeng. Thereafter the Red Turbans never recovered, and the Yuan court gained a brief respite.

v.t: Completion of the 'Spanish Chapel' frescoes in Italy
v.s: Andrea Bonaiuti completed the frescoes in the Spanish Chapel of Santa Maria Novella in Florence, representing the triumph of the Church and the flourishing of learning – a masterpiece of Gothic art.

v.t: Battle of Chizhou: Zhu Yuanzhang defeats Chen Youliang in preliminary to Lake Poyang
v.s: Chen Youliang, having killed Xu Shouhui and proclaimed himself emperor of the Great Han, sailed downriver with his fleet. Zhu Yuanzhang's general Chang Yuchun ambushed Chen at Chizhou, killing over 10,000. The curtain rose for their decisive battle.

v.t: Treaty of Brétigny signed, marking a truce in the Hundred Years' War
v.s: England and France signed the treaty: France ceded Calais, Ponthieu, and other large territories and paid 3 million gold crowns to ransom King John II; England gave up its claim to the French throne. However, war resumed nine years later.

v.t: Ottoman Empire captures Adrianople and makes it the capital
v.s: Murad I (son of Orhan) led his forces to capture the important Byzantine city of Adrianople (Edirne), making it the Ottoman capital and severing Constantinople's links to the Balkans. Ottoman power now pressed toward Central Europe.

v.t: Chaghan Temür assassinated; Yuan loses its last pillar
v.s: While suppressing Red Turbans in Shandong, Yuan general Chaghan Temür was assassinated by turncoats Tian Feng and Wang Shicheng. His adoptive son Köke Temür (Wang Baobao) inherited his forces. The Yuan generals began fighting among themselves, unable to focus on the south.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang fights the decisive Battle of Lake Poyang against Chen Youliang
v.s: Chen Youliang, with 600,000 men and giant tower ships, fought Zhu's 200,000 sailors on Lake Poyang. Zhu used fire ships, and the wind spread the flames, burning hundreds of Chen's vessels. Chen Youliang was killed by a stray arrow while trying to break out. The battle laid the foundation for Zhu's unification of the south.

v.t: First English-language tax return in England
v.s: In 1362, the English Parliament passed a law making English the language of court proceedings. That same year, the English government began using English (instead of Latin or French) for tax returns. This was a milestone in establishing English as an official language.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang captures Wuchang, ending Chen Youliang's remnants
v.s: Following his victory at Lake Poyang, Zhu Yuanzhang took Wuchang. Chen Youliang's son Chen Li surrendered. Zhu now controlled the Huguang region, greatly strengthening his power and setting the stage for unifying the whole country.

v.t: Malacca Sultanate founded
v.s: A prince of Srivijaya, Parameswara, fled from Majapahit to the mouth of the Malacca River. Witnessing a mouse deer turning on its attackers (a good omen), he founded a city there, later converting to Islam. Malacca quickly grew into Southeast Asia's most important trading port.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang proclaims himself Prince of Wu and establishes administrative system
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang assumed the title Prince of Wu in Yingtian, set up a bureaucracy with Li Shanchang as right chancellor and Xu Da as left chancellor, and issued a manifesto against Zhang Shicheng, clarifying his political goals.

v.t: Charles V crowned King of France, beginning the reconquest
v.s: King John II of France died in London, and his son Crown Prince Charles succeeded as Charles V. He employed Bertrand du Guesclin as constable, adopting scorched‑earth and guerrilla tactics to gradually recover territory lost to England.

v.t: Timur's Empire conquers Persia and rises
v.s: The Turco-Mongol Timur (Tamerlane) united the tribes of Transoxiana (present‑day Uzbekistan) and declared himself 'Great Emir' with Samarkand as his capital. Claiming descent from Genghis Khan, he began conquering Persia, eventually building a vast empire from India to Asia Minor.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang captures the Eastern Wu; siege of Pingjiang
v.s: In 1365 (Zhizheng 25), Zhu Yuanzhang sent Xu Da and Chang Yuchun to attack the Eastern Wu held by Zhang Shicheng. Pingjiang (present‑day Suzhou), Zhang's capital, was besieged for nearly a year before falling; Zhang Shicheng was captured and brought to Yingtian. Zhu now controlled the richest region south of the Yangtze.

v.t: Swiss Confederacy defeats the Habsburgs, consolidating independence
v.s: The eight cantons of the Swiss Confederacy decisively defeated the Austrian Habsburg army at the Battle of Sempach and concluded a peace, achieving de facto independence. The Swiss pike square became famous across Europe.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang destroys Zhang Shicheng and unifies the south
v.s: Pingjiang fell; Zhang Shicheng was captured and either committed suicide or was killed. Zhu moved wealthy families from Suzhou to Fengyang to consolidate his rule. The remaining major southern powers were now only Chen Youding in Fujian and He Zhen in Guangdong and Guangxi.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang issues the 'Proclamation to the Central Plains' and begins the northern expedition
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang appointed Xu Da as Grand Field Marshal and Chang Yuchun as deputy, leading 250,000 troops north. The proclamation, with the slogan 'Expel the barbarians and restore China', while also acknowledging Yuan legitimacy to appease Mongol nobles. The northern forces first took Shandong, then moved on Henan.

v.t: Hundred Years' War, Phase II: French counterattack
v.s: In 1367, the second phase of the Hundred Years' War (1369–1389) was about to begin. King Charles V of France reorganised the army and finances, appointed Bertrand du Guesclin as commander, and adopted a strategy of avoiding major battles while harassing the English, gradually recovering Poitou, Brittany, and other territories occupied by England.

v.t: Zhu Yuanzhang proclaims himself emperor in Yingtian, founding the Ming Dynasty
v.s: On the fourth day of the first lunar month, Zhu Yuanzhang ascended the throne in Yingtian with the dynastic title 'Great Ming' and era name Hongwu. He posthumously honoured his ancestors, made his son Zhu Biao crown prince, and appointed Li Shanchang and Xu Da as left and right chancellors. The Ming restored Han Chinese rule and abolished Yuan abuses.

v.t: Xu Da captures Dadu, ending the Yuan dynasty
v.s: The northern expedition went smoothly. In August, Xu Da's forces entered Dadu (Beijing). Emperor Shundi (Toghon Temür) fled north with his family, first to Shangdu and then to Yingchang, known as the 'Northern Yuan'. Yuan rule in China ended, and the Ming unified the north.

v.t: Ming issues the 'Hongwu Treasure Note'
v.s: The Ming issued the Great Ming Treasure Note to circulate alongside copper coins, forbidding the private use of gold and silver. However, over‑issuance led to rapid depreciation, causing people to use silver unofficially, foreshadowing later Ming monetary crises.

v.t: Ming forces capture Shanxi; Köke Temür retreats to Gansu
v.s: In 1369 (Hongwu 2), Xu Da and Chang Yuchun led Ming troops to capture Taiyuan. The Yuan general Köke Temür (Wang Baobao) was defeated and fled to Gansu. The Ming pacified Shanxi and Shaanxi, gaining control of most of the area north of the Yellow River, while Yuan remnants withdrew to the northern steppe.

v.t: Timur formally crowned, establishing the Timurid Empire
v.s: Timur convened a kurultai in Samarkand, where he was proclaimed 'Great Emir' (Amir), formally establishing his empire. He claimed to be the successor of Genghis Khan and began the conquest of Persia.

v.t: First imperial examination of the Ming dynasty
v.s: The Ming restored the imperial examinations, holding prefectural and metropolitan exams. Because most successful candidates came from the south, northern scholars protested. Emperor Taizu ordered a review but confirmed the original list, though thereafter instituted a northern‑southern quota system.

v.t: Ming northern expedition to Yingchang; Emperor Shundi dies
v.s: In 1370 (Hongwu 3), Li Wenzhong led Ming forces to capture Yingchang (present‑day Hexigten Banner, Inner Mongolia), a stronghold of the Yuan remnants. Emperor Shundi (Toghon Temür) had already died of dysentery; his son Ayushiridara fled to Karakorum (present‑day Kharkhorin, Mongolia), known as the Northern Yuan.

v.t: Ming pacifies Sichuan, unifying the mainland
v.s: Ming forces attacked and destroyed the Ming Xia regime (founded by Ming Yuzhen) by combined land and water assault. The Xia ruler Ming Sheng surrendered, and Sichuan came under Ming control. Except for Yunnan and Liaodong, the Ming had basically unified the territory of the former Yuan.

v.t: Polish‑Hungarian‑Lithuanian alliance against the Golden Horde
v.s: In 1371, the Golden Horde invaded Poland. King Casimir III of Poland, King Louis I of Hungary, and Grand Duke Algirdas of Lithuania united their forces to repel the Mongol‑Tatar invasion, strengthening the defensive system of Eastern Europe.

v.t: Ming three‑pronged northern expedition; heavy defeat at Lingbei
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang ordered Xu Da, Li Wenzhong, and Feng Sheng to lead three armies against the Northern Yuan. The central army under Xu Da was ambushed at Lingbei (present‑day Mongolia) by Köke Temür, losing tens of thousands. Li Wenzhong's eastern army also suffered reverses. Only the western army under Feng Sheng was victorious. The Ming realised they could not quickly destroy the Northern Yuan and turned to defence.

v.t: John Wycliffe proposes reformist ideas in England
v.s: John Wycliffe, a theology professor at Oxford, began to denounce papal corruption and argue that the Bible was the highest authority and that rituals should be simplified. He is often called the 'Morning Star of the Reformation'.

v.t: England and Portugal sign a permanent treaty of alliance
v.s: King Edward III of England and King Ferdinand I of Portugal signed the Anglo‑Portuguese Treaty, pledging mutual aid against Castile. The treaty remains in force today, making it the world's oldest active diplomatic alliance.

v.t: Ming promulgates the first version of the Great Ming Code
v.s: In 1373 (Hongwu 6), Zhu Yuanzhang ordered Li Shanchang, Liu Ji, and others to draft the Great Ming Code. After several revisions, a preliminary version was completed in 1374. Based on the Tang Code, it comprised 12 sections and 606 articles.

v.t: Boccaccio dies in Florence
v.s: Giovanni Boccaccio, author of The Decameron, died in Florence and was buried in the Church of San Jacopo. A close friend of Petrarch and one of the 'Three Crowns' of the Renaissance, he profoundly influenced the European short story.

v.t: Ming establishes the Paper Money Bureau to manage banknotes
v.s: In 1374 (Hongwu 7), Zhu Yuanzhang established the Paper Money Bureau to issue and manage the Great Ming Treasure Note. The notes, printed on mulberry‑bark paper and denominated from 100 wén to 1 guàn, were officially issued in 1375 (Hongwu 8). Zhu tried to impose a pure paper currency system, banning gold and silver, but the notes had no reserve and were over‑issued, leading to rapid depreciation. By the mid‑15th century, they were worth less than 10% of face value.

v.t: Ming founding figure Liu Ji (Liu Bowen) dies
v.s: Liu Ji died of illness in Nanjing; legend says he was poisoned by Hu Weiyong. Liu was Zhu Yuanzhang's chief strategist, helping to formulate military regulations and the legal code. Later generations often mythologised him as a sage strategist.

v.t: Catalan Atlas completed
v.s: Abraham Cresques of the Majorcan cartographic school finished the Catalan Atlas, blending Jewish, Arab, and Christian geographic knowledge. It depicts the world from the Atlantic to China and includes an image of Mansa Musa of Mali.

v.t: Ming abolishes the Branch Secretariats and establishes provincial administration commissions
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang abolished the Branch Secretariats (xingzhongshusheng) and set up three separate offices: the Provincial Administration Commission (finance/civil affairs), the Provincial Surveillance Commission (justice), and the Provincial Military Commission (military). This weakened local power and strengthened centralisation.

v.t: English Parliament opens with English for the first time
v.s: In 1376 (the end of Edward III's reign), the so‑called 'Good Parliament' used English for its opening speeches and debates for the first time, rather than French or Latin. Although the English nobility still mainly spoke French, English was already widespread among commoners. This event marked a milestone in the rise of the national language and reflected growing English national consciousness during the Hundred Years' War. The 1376 Parliament also introduced the impeachment procedure.

v.t: Pope Gregory XI moves the papacy from Avignon back to Rome
v.s: At the urging of Catherine of Siena, Pope Gregory XI left the French‑controlled Avignon and returned the papal court to Rome. However, most cardinals were still French, which led to the Western Schism.

v.t: Ming establishes the Transmission Office to manage petitions
v.s: In 1377 (Hongwu 10), Zhu Yuanzhang set up the Transmission Office (Tongzheng Shisi) to receive and deliver memorials and appeals between the court and local authorities. It was the central channel for official documents, but its powers were later taken over by the Grand Secretariat.

v.t: Western Schism: two popes in opposition
v.s: Gregory XI died, and the cardinals elected Urban VI (Italian). But the French cardinals claimed the election was coerced and elected Clement VII (French), returning to Avignon. The Church was split for 40 years, with different states supporting different popes, severely weakening papal authority.

v.t: Ming completes the conversion of provincial secretariats to administration commissions
v.s: In 1378 (Hongwu 11), Zhu Yuanzhang finalised the reform of provincial administration. The former branch secretariats were replaced by thirteen Provincial Administration Commissions, each responsible for civil and financial affairs. Separate Surveillance and Military Commissions handled justice and defence.

v.t: Timur invades Khwarazm, conquering Central Asia
v.s: Timur's forces conquered Khwarazm (south of the Aral Sea), captured its capital Urgench, massacred the population, and deported skilled craftsmen to Samarkand. The Timurid Empire now controlled the central section of the Silk Road.

v.t: Translation of the Wycliffe Bible into English begins
v.s: John Wycliffe's supporters began translating the Bible from Latin into English. This first complete English Bible (finished 1384) promoted religious reform.

v.t: Hu Weiyong case erupts; Zhu Yuanzhang abolishes the post of chancellor
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang executed Chancellor Hu Weiyong on charges of conspiracy and purged over 30,000 people. Taking advantage of the case, he abolished the Central Secretariat and the position of chancellor, ordering the six ministries to report directly to the emperor. Centralisation increased dramatically, but the emperor's workload became enormous.

v.t: Battle of Kulikovo: Muscovy defeats the Golden Horde
v.s: Grand Prince Dmitry Donskoy of Moscow led Russian forces to a major victory over Mamai's Golden Horde army, breaking the myth of Mongol invincibility. Although the Mongols sacked Moscow two years later, the battle was a turning point in Russia's liberation from Mongol rule.

v.t: Wat Tyler's Rebellion in England
v.s: The imposition of a poll tax triggered a massive uprising. Peasants from Kent and Essex, led by Wat Tyler and John Ball, marched on London, killing the Archbishop of Canterbury. The young King Richard II negotiated with the rebels; after the mayor of London killed Tyler, Richard II calmed the crowd by saying, 'I am your king.' The rebellion was later suppressed, but it accelerated the end of serfdom in England.

v.t: Ming pacifies Yunnan; Mu Ying takes command
v.s: Ming forces under Fu Youde, Lan Yu, and Mu Ying invaded Yunnan and defeated the Yuan Prince of Liang. Mu Ying remained as garrison commander, promoting military farming and Confucian education, formally integrating Yunnan into the Ming realm.

v.t: Ming establishes the Embroidered Uniform Guard (Jinyiwei)
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang created the Embroidered Uniform Guard as his personal bodyguard and secret service, in charge of prisons, arrests, and surveillance. Reporting directly to the emperor, the Jinyiwei became a menacing feature of Ming politics.

v.t: Empress Ma dies
v.s: In 1382 (Hongwu 15), Zhu Yuanzhang's wife, Empress Ma, died. She was his first wife and known for her virtue and kindness, often persuading Zhu to be merciful and saving many officials from his wrath. Her death deeply grieved Zhu Yuanzhang.

v.t: Portuguese‑Castilian War (1383–1385)
v.s: King Ferdinand I of Portugal died without an heir, sparking a war with Castile over the succession. The Portuguese rallied around John of Aviz (later John I) to resist the Castilian invasion.

v.t: Ming issues the Yellow Register of Taxes and Corvée
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang ordered the nationwide compilation of household registers, prepared in duplicate (one yellow register kept at the Ministry of Revenue, the other locally). The Yellow Register system became the core of Ming household administration for tax purposes.

v.t: Chaucer begins writing The Canterbury Tales
v.s: English poet Geoffrey Chaucer began writing The Canterbury Tales, a frame story of pilgrims telling tales on a journey to Canterbury, depicting the social spectrum of medieval England. Written in the London dialect, it had a profound influence on English literature.

v.t: Ming makes Nanjing the capital and abolishes the Beiping Administration
v.s: In 1384 (Hongwu 17), Zhu Yuanzhang formally established Nanjing (Yingtian) as the capital, and the Beiping Administration was dissolved, with its territory directly administered by the Central Secretariat. After twenty years of construction, Nanjing's walls had a perimeter of about 35 km and a population of about 500,000, making it one of the world's largest cities. The imperial palace and walls laid out the capital pattern of the Ming for nearly 300 years.

v.t: Polish‑Lithuanian union formally established (Union of Krewo)
v.s: Queen Jadwiga of Poland married Grand Duke Jogaila (Władysław II Jagiełło) of Lithuania. Jogaila was baptised as a Catholic and became King of Poland. The union created a single state to oppose the Teutonic Knights, leading to the Polish‑Lithuanian Commonwealth 400 years later.

v.t: Ming's Empty Seal case: massive punishment of officials
v.s: In 1385 (Hongwu 18), Zhu Yuanzhang discovered that local officials, when submitting financial accounts to the Ministry of Revenue, had used pre‑sealed blank documents ('empty seals'). He considered this collective fraud. A strict investigation followed; hundreds of officials were executed or exiled. It was one of the 'Four Major Cases' of the early Ming.

v.t: Heidelberg University founded
v.s: Rupert I, Elector Palatine of the Holy Roman Empire, founded Heidelberg University with a papal bull. It is the oldest university in the German‑speaking lands and became a centre of European humanism.

v.t: Ming implements the lijia (village) system
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang ordered the nationwide establishment of lijia: every 110 households formed one li, with a li head; each li was divided into jia of 10 households. This grassroots administration lasted until the end of the Qing.

v.t: Ming campaigns against Liaodong; Nahacu surrenders
v.s: In 1387 (Hongwu 20), Zhu Yuanzhang sent Feng Sheng and Fu Youde with 200,000 troops against Nahacu, a Yuan remnant leader in Liaodong. Isolated, Nahacu surrendered with over 200,000 men. The Ming unified the northeast and established the Liaodong Regional Military Commission.

v.t: Prelude to the fall of Goryeo: Yi Seong‑gye rises
v.s: After turning back at Wihwa Island, General Yi Seong‑gye came to dominate the Goryeo military. He carried out land reform, eliminated political enemies like Choe Yeong, and gradually seized control of the Goryeo government. In 1392, he deposed the Goryeo king and founded the Joseon dynasty.

v.t: Ming victory at Lake Buir; Northern Yuan reduced to a rump
v.s: Lan Yu led 150,000 Ming troops in a surprise attack on the Northern Yuan court at Lake Buir (Lake Buyr), capturing the second son of the khan, 7,000 palace women, and 150,000 head of livestock. The Northern Yuan khan fled and was killed by his own men. Mongolia split into Tatar and Oirat confederations. The Ming's northern threat was greatly reduced.

v.t: Joseon dynasty founded: Yi Seong‑gye deposes Goryeo
v.s: Yi Seong‑gye deposed King Gongyang of Goryeo and took the throne himself, renaming the country Joseon and making Hanyang (Seoul) the capital. He sent envoys to the Ming asking for recognition; the Hongwu Emperor granted the name 'Joseon'. Yi pushed reforms, advocating Confucianism and suppressing Buddhism.

v.t: Battle of Kosovo: Ottomans defeat Serbia; both sultan and prince killed
v.s: Ottoman Sultan Murad I faced a Balkan coalition led by Prince Lazar of Serbia on the Kosovo Plain. Murad was assassinated by the Serbian knight Miloš Obilić, but the Serbian army was routed and Lazar was captured and executed. Serbia became an Ottoman vassal, and the Balkans lay open.

v.t: Ming finalises the Six Ministries structure
v.s: In 1389 (Hongwu 22), Zhu Yuanzhang fixed the structure of the Six Ministries (Personnel, Revenue, Rites, War, Justice, Works). Each ministry was responsible for its respective domain, and their ministers reported directly to the emperor.

v.t: Lan Yu case erupts; most of the founding generals are purged
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang executed the Duke of Liang, Lan Yu, on charges of treason, implicating Fu Youde, Feng Sheng, and other meritorious generals. Some 15,000 people were executed. After this, apart from Geng Bingwen, all the founding generals of the Ming were killed.

v.t: Black powder cannon comes into widespread use in Europe
v.s: During the Hundred Years' War, both England and France began using large numbers of bronze or iron cannons for siege warfare. The fall of Calais, Cherbourg, and other towns was attributed to artillery. The gunpowder revolution changed the nature of warfare.

v.t: Kingdom of Kongo established
v.s: Around 1390, the Kingdom of Kongo was founded along the lower Congo River. The king (manikongo) controlled territory from the Atlantic coast inland. When the Portuguese arrived in 1483, King Afonso I (c.1506–1543) converted to Christianity and adopted European technology, trying to modernise. The slave trade with Portugal eventually led to the kingdom's decline. Kongo was one of the first sub‑Saharan African states to have close diplomatic contact with Europe.

v.t: Bayezid I succeeds as Ottoman sultan, accelerating Balkan conquest
v.s: Murad I was killed at Kosovo; his son Bayezid I (the 'Thunderbolt') succeeded. He swiftly conquered Bulgaria, Macedonia, and Thessaly, blockading Constantinople.

v.t: Ming assigns princes to their appanages
v.s: In 1391 (Hongwu 24), Zhu Yuanzhang enfeoffed his adult sons as regional princes. They had certain military powers and influence, especially the 'border princes' in the north. Zhu Di, Prince of Yan (later the Yongle Emperor), was enfeoffed at Beiping.

v.t: Joseon dynasty officially receives Ming recognition
v.s: In 1392 (Hongwu 25), Yi Seong‑gye formally replaced Goryeo and founded the Joseon dynasty. He sent envoys to Nanjing to request recognition. The Hongwu Emperor bestowed the name 'Joseon' and recognised Yi as 'Advisor to the Joseon State'. The Chinese‑Korean tributary relationship was thus established.

v.t: Crown Prince Zhu Biao dies; Zhu Yunwen made imperial grandson
v.s: In 1392, Crown Prince Zhu Biao, the eldest son of Zhu Yuanzhang, died of illness after a tour of Shaanxi. Grief‑stricken, the emperor made Zhu Biao's son Zhu Yunwen the imperial grandson. The emperor began to eliminate potential threats to the young heir and to reduce the power of the princes.

v.t: Ming issues the Hongwu Ritual Regulations
v.s: In 1393 (Hongwu 26), the Hongwu Ritual Regulations, compiled by Li Shanchang, Liu Ji, and other Confucian scholars, were formally issued. The work prescribed clothing, marriage, funerary rites, sacrifices, and court ceremonies for the imperial family, officials, and commoners. Through these regulations, Zhu Yuanzhang sought to reinforce social hierarchy and Confucian ethics, creating a comprehensive ritual system from court to countryside.

v.t: Hanseatic League at its peak around the Baltic Sea
v.s: In 1393, the Hanseatic League reached the height of its power. Comprising free cities such as Lübeck, Hamburg, and Bremen, the League controlled northern European trade from London to Novgorod, possessing its own navy and diplomatic authority.

v.t: Joseon moves capital to Hanyang (Seoul)
v.s: In 1394 (Hongwu 27), King Taejo of Joseon (Yi Seong‑gye) moved the capital from Kaegyong (Kaesong) to Hanyang (present‑day Seoul). The new capital was laid out according to geomancy (feng shui) principles, with Gyeongbokgung Palace, the Royal Ancestral Shrine, and the Altars of Land and Grain. Seoul remained the capital of Joseon for over 500 years.

v.t: Ming strictly forbids eunuch interference, sets up an iron tablet
v.s: In 1394 (Hongwu 27), recalling the scourge of eunuchs in previous dynasties, Zhu Yuanzhang erected an iron tablet at the palace gate inscribed: 'Eunuchs shall not interfere in government; violators will be beheaded.' He ruled that eunuchs should not learn to read, not hold external offices, and not have private dealings with officials. Nevertheless, in the mid‑to‑late Ming (especially after Yongle), distrust of the scholar‑official class led emperors to rely on eunuchs. The iron prohibition became a dead letter.

v.t: Timur conquers the Golden Horde, sacks Sarai
v.s: Timur campaigned against the Golden Horde, capturing and burning its capital Sarai (near present‑day Volgograd). The Golden Horde never recovered and fragmented into several khanates.

v.t: Ming issues regulations for royal‑meritocratic intermarriage
v.s: In 1395 (Hongwu 28), Zhu Yuanzhang laid down rules for marriage between the imperial family and the families of meritorious officials. He stressed that princesses should marry the sons of meritorious officials to strengthen ties, while also forbidding the influence of consort families.

v.t: Nicopolis Crusade ends in disaster
v.s: The Duke of Burgundy organised a last crusade to relieve Constantinople. At Nicopolis (present‑day Bulgaria), it was routed by Bayezid I; thousands of knights were captured or killed, shocking Europe.

v.t: Ming compiles the 'Duties of the Various Offices', standardising bureaucracy
v.s: In 1396 (Hongwu 29), Zhu Yuanzhang ordered the compilation of the 'Duties of the Various Offices', which detailed the functions and procedures of central and local agencies. This was an important Ming administrative document, providing a standard model for later officials.

v.t: Timur invades India, sacks Delhi
v.s: Timur led 90,000 cavalry across the Hindu Kush to attack the Delhi Sultanate. Near Delhi, he defeated Sultan Mahmud Shah, entered Delhi, and plundered the city. Timur had no intention of occupying India and withdrew after looting.

v.t: Northern‑Southern List case: Zhu Yuanzhang strikes at Jiangnan gentry
v.s: In the metropolitan exam, all 52 successful candidates were from the south. Northern candidates rioted. To balance north and south, Zhu executed the chief examiners, held a new exam, and enrolled only northerners. This led to the later northern‑southern quota system.

v.t: Hongwu Emperor dies; Imperial Grandson Zhu Yunwen ascends the throne
v.s: Zhu Yuanzhang died in Nanjing at age 70. His will named the Imperial Grandson Zhu Yunwen as successor (the Jianwen Emperor). Zhu Yunwen immediately began reducing the power of the princes, prompting Zhu Di, Prince of Yan, to raise an army.

v.t: Oirat Mongols rise, kill the Tatar khan
v.s: In 1398, the Oirat (Western Mongols) under Ugechi Khashikha attacked and killed the Eastern Mongol (Tatar) khan Elbeg. The Oirats temporarily unified the Mongol tribes, becoming a new powerful threat on the Ming northern border.

v.t: Jingnan Rebellion (靖难之役) breaks out; Zhu Di revolts against the Jianwen Emperor
v.s: The Jianwen Emperor reduced the power of the princes, causing several to die. Zhu Di, Prince of Yan, raised troops in Beiping under the slogan 'clear the monarch's side' (jingnan). The Jianwen Emperor sent the veteran general Geng Bingwen, who was initially defeated. He then replaced him with Li Jinglong, who was also defeated by Zhu Di. The rebellion lasted three years.

v.t: House of Lancaster established in England
v.s: In 1399, Richard II of England was deposed by Henry Bolingbroke, Duke of Lancaster. Henry assumed the throne as Henry IV in parliament, founding the House of Lancaster. This event was a prelude to the Wars of the Roses (1455–1487).

v.t: Battle of Baihe River (Jingnan Rebellion): Zhu Di turns the tide
v.s: Li Jinglong, with 600,000 men, fought Zhu Di's 100,000 at the Baihe River. Zhu Di was nearly captured, but a sudden wind broke Li's command flag, and the southern army collapsed. Li Jinglong was utterly defeated, and Zhu Di shifted from defence to offence.

v.t: Chaucer dies, buried in Westminster Abbey's Poets' Corner
v.s: Geoffrey Chaucer, father of English poetry, died. He was the first poet buried in Poets' Corner at Westminster Abbey. The Canterbury Tales created the English literary tradition.

v.t: French writer Christine de Pizan composes The Book of the City of Ladies
v.s: Christine de Pizan, a pioneer of French feminism, wrote The Book of the City of Ladies to refute misogyny and advocate for women's education and virtue. She was the first European woman to earn a living as a writer.

v.t: Timur prepares an expedition against the Ming but dies en route (actually 1405)
v.s: Timur assembled 200,000 troops in Samarkand for an expedition against the Ming, but his death in 1405 (due to old age and severe winter) prevented it. If he had succeeded, East Asian history might have been different.

v.t: Woodblock printing has reached Europe; Gutenberg not yet active
v.s: Around 1400, European artisans began using woodblock printing to produce playing cards, religious images, and single‑leaf book pages. The technique was brought from China via the Silk Road by travellers like Marco Polo. Workshops were established in France, Italy, and Germany, accumulating technical experience that would lead to Gutenberg's movable type half a century later. The combination of block printing and movable type sparked an information revolution and the Reformation.

v.t: Easter Island moai statue construction at its peak
v.s: Between the 12th and 15th centuries (c.1200–1500), the Polynesian inhabitants of Rapa Nui (Easter Island) reached the peak of moai statue construction. About 900 statues were carved at the Rano Raraku quarry; some are 10 metres tall and weigh up to 82 tonnes. Deforestation and overexploitation of resources eventually led to societal collapse.

v.t: Olmec civilization: the mother culture of Mesoamerica
v.s: From roughly 1400 to 400 BCE, the Olmec civilization flourished along the Gulf Coast of Mexico (present‑day Veracruz and Tabasco). The Olmec carved colossal basalt heads (up to 3 metres high and 20‑50 tonnes), developed a calendar and the earliest writing in Mesoamerica, and worshipped the jaguar god and the feathered serpent. San Lorenzo and La Venta served as political and religious centres. The Olmecs are recognised as the 'mother culture' of Mesoamerica, influencing the Maya, Zapotec, and Aztec civilisations.
